title: freqTLS Roadmap
text: Building beyond v0.1 core, complementary (competitive) framing bayesTLS – two packages two valid lenses model. Parametric bootstrap CIs – done. Prior-free percentile intervals via confint(method = "bootstrap"), automatic fallback non-closing profile non-positive-definite Hessian (fallback = TRUE). freqTLS now attempts finite interval without prior; unstable bootstrap fits return explicit NA rather fabricating bounds. Canonical bayesTLS comparator cache – done. Built Totoro pinned bayesTLS commit 76510412, independently published exact SHA-256, checked exact data/formulas/thresholds plus passing sampler diagnostics. comparison article reports actual point differences without treating posterior medians ML estimates identical targets. Random intercept CTmax – done. CTmax ~ <fixed> + (1 | group) via TMB Laplace; -RE path byte-identical; sigma_CTmax reported (ML, biased low groups); ranef() BLUPs; profile-likelihood intervals fixed effects random effect (Laplace re-run grid point; sigma_CTmax, bootstrap, Confidence Eye stay Wald). Beta (continuous-proportion) family – done. family = "beta" fits response proportion (0, 1) directly (y ~ Beta(p * phi, (1 - p) * phi)), trials n required; point, Wald, profile (including phi), bootstrap intervals work, grouped CTmax/z supported. Critical-temperature derivations – done. derive_ctmax() (absolute threshold) derive_tcrit() (rate-multiplier T_crit = CTmax + z*log10(rate/100), lethal-endpoint ) complete bayesTLS extract_tdt() absolute family ML, deterministic transforms fitted CTmax/z. Heat-injury prediction – done. predict_heat_injury() deterministically accumulates thermal damage fitted curve temperature trace (ML analogue bayesTLS::predict_heat_injury()), optional damage cutoff optional (unidentified) Sharpe-Schoolfield repair layer. One lethal dose maps relative midpoint survival default, absolute survival threshold via target_surv. predicts fitted curve; fitting injury/repair models stays bayesTLS concern. Covariate (grouped) effects shape parameters – done. low / / log_k can vary grouping factor formula interface, relaxing midpoint-invariant (engine carries shape design matrices; intercept-byte-identical). Per-group estimates + Wald intervals + predict(); simulate_tls() accepts per-group shapes. General continuous covariates shapes – done. shape may carry design independently (continuous covariate, grouping factor, intercept), longer constrained share one design match CTmax / log_z grouping. Per-shape engine widths (byte-identical default), link-scale coefficient estimates (k:body_size log-scale slope) Wald intervals, predict() rebuilds shape design newdata. ## Historical implementation milestone (shipped experimental 0.1.0) Random intercept log_z (item 5) — done. log_z ~ <fixed> + (1 | group) adds random intercept thermal sensitivity, symmetric counterpart CTmax intercept (engine b_logz / log_sd_logz / re_index_logz, -RE path byte-identical). sigma_logz ML SD log(z) (multiplicative spread z, biased low groups); ranef() returns log_z BLUPs; fixed effects profile Laplace; RE-aware bootstrap redraws deviations. CTmax log_z intercepts combine (different grouping); grouping fits two independent variances warns. See docs/design/08-random-effects.md. Random intercepts shape coordinates low log_k — done. low ~ <fixed> + (1 | group) log_k ~ <fixed> + (1 | group) complete “random effects sub-parameter,” REs available CTmax / log_z / low / log_k (engine b_low / b_logk, byte-identical -RE path). sigma_low SD logit(low), sigma_logk log(k) (ML). upper asymptote excluded (compiled objective random-intercept term ). -grouping independent-variance warning generalised across four coordinates. dedicated vignette("random-effects") walks lot. Heat-injury bootstrap envelope — done. heat_injury_envelope() returns prior-free parametric-bootstrap confidence band around predict_heat_injury() survival trajectory (likelihood-path analogue bayesTLS posterior survival band), reusing dose-accumulation integrator; plot_heat_injury() draws . Planned: random effects still — second / crossed / nested grouping factor one sub-parameter, random slopes, correlated multivariate random effects across coordinates. need stacked-random-vector engine redesign; bayesTLS remains path correlated random structure.

what: Main analysis: heat branch at age 6
title: Case 2: cereal-aphid thermal tolerance
text: beta-binomial model gives species direct CTmax z, lets steepness vary centred assay temperature, shares low . t_ref = 60 one hour standardized duration unit minutes. experimental freqTLS page reports CTmax z . pinned bayesTLS case additionally extracts Tcrit; users needing Bayesian derived quantity use supplement rather interpret omission zero biological effect.  table reports Wald confidence intervals. TDT figure shows fitted point curves, uncertainty intervals. Check convergence, Hessian status, gradient, data-adequacy warnings ranking species.
code: aphid6 <- droplevels(subset(aphid_tdt, branch == "heat" & age == "6")) aphid6$species <- factor(   aphid6$species,   levels = c("M_dirhodum", "R_padi", "S_avenae"),   labels = c("Metopolophium dirhodum", "Rhopalosiphum padi",              "Sitobion avenae") ) aphid6_std <- standardize_data(   aphid6, temp = "temp", duration = "duration_min",   n_total = "n_total", n_surv = "n_surv", duration_unit = "minutes" ) aphid6_fit <- fit_4pl(   aphid6_std,   ctmax = ~ 0 + species,   z = ~ 0 + species,   low = ~ 1,   up = ~ 1,   k = ~ temp_c,   family = "beta_binomial",   t_ref = 60,   method = "wald",   quiet = TRUE ) diagnose_tdt_fit(aphid6_fit) #> # A tibble: 1 × 9 #>   converged pd_hessian max_abs_gradient gradient_pass optimizer  logLik n_params #>   <lgl>     <lgl>                 <dbl> <lgl>         <chr>       <dbl>    <int> #> 1 TRUE      TRUE              0.0000293 TRUE          nloptr_TN…  -760.       11 #> # ℹ 2 more variables: AIC <dbl>, all_pass <lgl> aphid6_tls <- tls(aphid6_fit, by = "species", lethal = FALSE, method = "wald") aphid6_table <- aphid6_tls$summary names(aphid6_table)[names(aphid6_table) == "median"] <- "estimate" aphid6_table #> # A tibble: 6 × 5 #>   species                quantity estimate lower upper #>   <chr>                  <chr>       <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 Metopolophium dirhodum CTmax       35.2  35.0  35.4  #> 2 Rhopalosiphum padi     CTmax       37.2  37.1  37.3  #> 3 Sitobion avenae        CTmax       36.5  36.4  36.6  #> 4 Metopolophium dirhodum z            4.75  4.51  5.00 #> 5 Rhopalosiphum padi     z            3.97  3.70  4.25 #> 6 Sitobion avenae        z            3.61  3.46  3.77 plot_tdt_curve(aphid6_fit$fit)

title: Case 2: cereal-aphid thermal tolerance

code: aphid_all <- droplevels(subset(aphid_tdt, branch == "heat")) aphid_all$species <- factor(   aphid_all$species,   levels = c("M_dirhodum", "R_padi", "S_avenae"),   labels = c("Metopolophium dirhodum", "Rhopalosiphum padi",              "Sitobion avenae") ) aphid_all_std <- standardize_data(   aphid_all, temp = "temp", duration = "duration_min",   n_total = "n_total", n_surv = "n_surv", duration_unit = "minutes" ) aphid_all_fit <- fit_4pl(   aphid_all_std,   ctmax = ~ 1 + species * age,   z = ~ 1 + species * age,   low = ~ 1,   up = ~ 1,   k = ~ temp_c,   family = "beta_binomial",   t_ref = 60,   method = "wald",   quiet = TRUE ) diagnose_tdt_fit(aphid_all_fit) #> # A tibble: 1 × 9 #>   converged pd_hessian max_abs_gradient gradient_pass optimizer  logLik n_params #>   <lgl>     <lgl>                 <dbl> <lgl>         <chr>       <dbl>    <int> #> 1 TRUE      TRUE              0.0000165 TRUE          nloptr_TN… -2303.       23 #> # ℹ 2 more variables: AIC <dbl>, all_pass <lgl> aphid_cells <- expand.grid(   species = levels(aphid_all$species),   age = levels(aphid_all$age),   temp = mean(aphid_all_std$temp),   KEEP.OUT.ATTRS = FALSE ) aphid_cell_pars <- predict(aphid_all_fit, aphid_cells, type = "parameters") cbind(aphid_cells[c("species", "age")],       aphid_cell_pars[c("CTmax", "z")]) #>                  species age    CTmax        z #> 1 Metopolophium dirhodum   2 35.10379 4.801607 #> 2     Rhopalosiphum padi   2 36.68836 3.384634 #> 3        Sitobion avenae   2 36.00785 4.169478 #> 4 Metopolophium dirhodum   6 35.22167 4.717234 #> 5     Rhopalosiphum padi   6 37.17018 3.842124 #> 6        Sitobion avenae   6 36.53607 3.564974 #> 7 Metopolophium dirhodum  12 35.99535 3.999057 #> 8     Rhopalosiphum padi  12 36.92917 3.890682 #> 9        Sitobion avenae  12 36.58300 3.275348 aphid_tdt_grid <- merge(   expand.grid(     temp = seq(min(aphid_all_std$temp), max(aphid_all_std$temp), length.out = 60),     KEEP.OUT.ATTRS = FALSE   ),   aphid_cells[c("species", "age")] ) aphid_tdt_pars <- predict(aphid_all_fit, aphid_tdt_grid, type = "parameters") aphid_tdt_grid$duration_50 <- 10^(   log10(60) - (aphid_tdt_grid$temp - aphid_tdt_pars$CTmax) / aphid_tdt_pars$z ) ggplot2::ggplot(   aphid_tdt_grid,   ggplot2::aes(temp, duration_50, colour = species) ) +   ggplot2::geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   ggplot2::scale_y_log10() +   ggplot2::facet_wrap(~ age) +   ggplot2::labs(     x = "Temperature (°C)",     y = "Duration to 50% relative survival (minutes, log scale)",     colour = "Species"   ) +   ggplot2::theme_minimal()

title: Cross-checking freqTLS with bayesTLS
text: primary table puts maximum-likelihood estimate Wald confidence interval beside Bayesian posterior median credible interval. Interval widths descriptive, method-specific quantities: confidence-interval width credible-interval width interchangeable measures uncertainty. Matched quantities: ML Wald confidence intervals Bayesian credible intervals remain labelled separately. age-six aphid row also one cell -age extension, two fits intentionally distinct. nine species--age cells, largest absolute freqTLS-minus-bayesTLS point difference reported separately rather printing another 18-row table. Snow-gum paired refit locked shared-shape analogue. claim freqTLS reproduces richer recovery--temperature shape model displayed pinned supplement.
code: as_freq_rows <- function(fit, case_id, by, endpoint, t_ref) {   out <- tls(fit, by = by, lethal = FALSE, method = "wald")$summary   data.frame(     case_id = case_id,     endpoint = endpoint,     threshold = "relative",     t_ref = t_ref,     group = as.character(out[[by]]),     parameter = out$quantity,     freq_estimate = out$median,     freq_lower = out$lower,     freq_upper = out$upper,     freq_interval = "Wald 95% confidence interval"   ) }  freq_rows <- rbind(   as_freq_rows(     zf_fit, "zebrafish_oxygen", "oxygen", "survival counts", 60   ),   as_freq_rows(     aphid6_fit, "aphid_age6", "species", "survival counts", 60   ),   as_freq_rows(     snowgum_fit, "snowgum_psii", "recovery",     "retained PSII function proportion", 60   ),   as_freq_rows(     coma_fit, "drosophila_awake", "sex",     "awake counts (missing t_coma)", 60   ) )  bayes_rows <- bayes_cache$summaries bayes_rows$group <- ifelse(   !is.na(bayes_rows$oxygen), as.character(bayes_rows$oxygen),   ifelse(     !is.na(bayes_rows$recovery), as.character(bayes_rows$recovery),     ifelse(       !is.na(bayes_rows$sex), as.character(bayes_rows$sex),       as.character(bayes_rows$species)     )   ) )  paired <- merge(   freq_rows,   bayes_rows[c(     "case_id", "endpoint", "threshold", "t_ref", "group", "parameter",     "median", "lower", "upper", "interval_method"   )],   by = c("case_id", "endpoint", "threshold", "t_ref", "group", "parameter"),   all = FALSE,   sort = FALSE ) names(paired)[names(paired) == "median"] <- "bayes_median" names(paired)[names(paired) == "lower"] <- "bayes_lower" names(paired)[names(paired) == "upper"] <- "bayes_upper" names(paired)[names(paired) == "interval_method"] <- "bayes_interval" paired$difference_freq_minus_bayes <- paired$freq_estimate - paired$bayes_median paired$freq_width <- paired$freq_upper - paired$freq_lower paired$bayes_width <- paired$bayes_upper - paired$bayes_lower stopifnot(nrow(paired) == 18L)  paired_display <- paired[c(   "case_id", "endpoint", "threshold", "t_ref", "group", "parameter",   "freq_estimate", "freq_lower", "freq_upper", "bayes_median",   "bayes_lower", "bayes_upper", "freq_width", "bayes_width",   "difference_freq_minus_bayes" )] names(paired_display) <- c(   "Case", "Endpoint", "Threshold", "tref", "Group", "Quantity",   "ML estimate", "ML CI lower", "ML CI upper", "Bayes median",   "Bayes CrI lower", "Bayes CrI upper", "ML CI width", "Bayes CrI width",   "ML minus Bayes" ) knitr::kable(paired_display, digits = 3,              caption = "Matched quantities: ML Wald confidence intervals and Bayesian credible intervals remain labelled separately.") aphid_cells <- expand.grid(   species = levels(aphid_all$species),   age = levels(aphid_all$age),   temp = mean(aphid_all_std$temp),   KEEP.OUT.ATTRS = FALSE ) aphid_pred <- predict(aphid_all_fit, aphid_cells, type = "parameters") aphid_freq <- rbind(   data.frame(     species = aphid_cells$species, age = aphid_cells$age,     parameter = "CTmax", freq_estimate = aphid_pred$CTmax   ),   data.frame(     species = aphid_cells$species, age = aphid_cells$age,     parameter = "z", freq_estimate = aphid_pred$z   ) ) aphid_bayes <- subset(bayes_rows, case_id == "aphid_all_age") aphid_join <- merge(   aphid_freq,   aphid_bayes[c("species", "age", "parameter", "median")],   by = c("species", "age", "parameter") ) aphid_join$difference_freq_minus_bayes <-   aphid_join$freq_estimate - aphid_join$median stopifnot(nrow(aphid_join) == 18L) aggregate(   abs(difference_freq_minus_bayes) ~ parameter,   data = aphid_join,   FUN = max ) #>   parameter abs(difference_freq_minus_bayes) #> 1     CTmax                       0.02725904 #> 2         z                       0.02246866

code: mort_lt50 <- data.frame(   group = mort_fit$fit$group_levels,   freq_estimate = vapply(mort_fit$fit$group_levels, function(sex_level) {     temp_grid <- seq(min(mort$temp), max(mort$temp), length.out = 1001)     grid_difference <- predict(       mort_fit,       data.frame(temp = temp_grid, duration = 240, group = sex_level),       type = "survival"     ) - 0.5     crossing <- which(       grid_difference[-length(grid_difference)] *         grid_difference[-1L] <= 0     )     stopifnot(length(crossing) == 1L)     objective <- function(temp) {       predict(         mort_fit,         data.frame(temp = temp, duration = 240, group = sex_level),         type = "survival"       ) - 0.5     }     uniroot(       objective,       temp_grid[c(crossing, crossing + 1L)],       tol = 1e-10     )$root   }, numeric(1)) ) mort_bayes <- subset(   bayes_rows,   case_id == "drosophila_mortality" & parameter == "CTmax" ) mort_compare <- merge(   mort_lt50,   mort_bayes[c("group", "median", "lower", "upper")],   by = "group" ) names(mort_compare)[names(mort_compare) == "median"] <- "bayes_median" mort_compare$difference_freq_minus_bayes <-   mort_compare$freq_estimate - mort_compare$bayes_median mort_compare$freq_interval <- "none: exact-model bootstrap unstable" stopifnot(nrow(mort_compare) == 2L) mort_compare #>   group freq_estimate bayes_median    lower    upper #> 1     F      35.15584     35.13553 35.01181 35.24284 #> 2     M      35.17396     35.16390 35.04825 35.26617 #>   difference_freq_minus_bayes                        freq_interval #> 1                  0.02031098 none: exact-model bootstrap unstable #> 2                  0.01005650 none: exact-model bootstrap unstable

title: Getting started with freqTLS
text: tls_bf() grammar scales single fit grouped, covariate, hierarchical models. families. Besides "beta_binomial", fit_tls() accepts "binomial" (overdispersion) "beta" (continuous proportion (0, 1) trials column; runnable example uses simulated beta data). Grouped CTmax z. Put grouping factor sub-parameter estimate separate, directly profile-able CTmax z per group, one shared curve shape (bayesTLS constant-shape configuration). formula, z enters internal log scale log_z (steepness log_k), grouped term written log_z ~ group. Intervals still requested natural-scale name (z:cool, log_z:cool) — confint() converts internally: Shape predictors. shape parameters longer intercept-: low, , log_k take grouping factor continuous covariate (example low ~ group log_k ~ body_size). See ?tls_bf. random intercept. what: The blurry line: clamping, shrinkage, and penalties as implicit priors
title: Frequentist and Bayesian thermal-load sensitivity: strengths, weaknesses, and the blurry line
text: tempting frame “prior versus prior”. clean. Frequentist fits routinely embed prior-like regularisation, freqTLS exception. Three forms appear package. Clamping. keep likelihood well behaved, freqTLS clamps fitted probability boundary ([1e-12, 1 - 1e-12]), clamps beta- family response {0, 1}, floors Beta shape parameters. hard interior bound — behaviourally degenerate prior assigns mass edge. disease separation cures penalty weak prior: perfectly separated binary data unpenalised maximum- likelihood estimate runs infinity, mild penalty (equivalently, weakly informative prior) brings back finite value (Firth, 1993; Gelman, Jakulin, Pittau & Su, 2008). advantage numerical stability finite estimates; cost small, usually hidden, boundary bias arbitrary clamp constant. Shrinkage / partial pooling. freqTLS’s random intercepts CTmax, log_z, low, log_k (e.g. CTmax ~ <fixed> + (1 | group)) assume group deviations Gaussian, b_g ~ N(0, sigma). Gaussian prior deviations, predicted group effects (BLUPs returned ranef()) shrunk toward zero exactly posterior mean — empirical-Bayes view mixed models (James & Stein, 1961; Efron & Morris, 1975; Robinson, 1991). Shrinkage borrows strength across groups lowers variance; costs shrinkage scale modelling assumption, maximum-likelihood variance biased low groups, boundary problem usually addressed — — penalty prior variance component (Chung, Rabe-Hesketh, Dorie, Gelman & Liu, 2013). spread colony effects pulled -pooling fit (separate CTmax per colony) shrunk random-effect BLUPs — Gaussian prior b_g ~ N(0, sigma) exactly prior . pull strongest data-poor colonies; many assays per colony little shrink. Penalised likelihood general. broadly, adding penalty log- likelihood maximum posteriori (MAP) estimation prior: ridge Gaussian prior (Hoerl & Kennard, 1970), lasso Laplace prior (Tibshirani, 1996; Park & Casella, 2008), Firth’s bias-reduction penalty Jeffreys prior (Firth, 1993). continuum maximum likelihood profile likelihood penalised likelihood laid Cole, Chu & Greenland (2014), penalty--prior equivalence — penalised likelihood equals maximum posteriori (MAP) estimation prior — standard (Bishop, 2006; Hastie, Tibshirani & Friedman, 2009). real distinction prior versus prior. regularisation expressed propagated: Bayesian prior explicit uncertainty flows posterior; clamp implicit fixed (tuning constant, propagated); random effect penalty semi-explicit (structural assumption scale estimated chosen). freqTLS keeps regularisation light transparent — documented clamp, optional clearly labelled random effect — reports confidence intervals rather posterior. honest say lightly regularised, assumption-free.
code: # Data-poor colonies (few assays each) so partial pooling has work to do. d_re <- simulate_tls(   family = "binomial", temps = c(34, 36, 38), times = c(1, 4), reps = 1, n = 8,   CTmax = 36, z = 4, re_sd = 1.5, n_re_groups = 12, seed = 42 ) # Partial pooling: a random intercept shrinks the colony effects toward 0. fit_re <- suppressWarnings(fit_tls(   tls_bf(survived | trials(total) ~ time(duration) + temp(temp),          CTmax ~ 1 + (1 | colony)),   data = d_re, family = "binomial", tref = 1 )) blup <- ranef(fit_re)$estimate # No pooling: a fixed CTmax per colony (the unshrunk per-group estimates). fit_fix <- suppressWarnings(fit_tls(   d_re, y = survived, n = total, time = duration, temp = temp,   group = colony, family = "binomial", tref = 1 )) ct_fix <- fit_fix$estimates$estimate[startsWith(fit_fix$estimates$parameter, "CTmax:")] # The Gaussian prior on b_g (scale sigma_CTmax) pulls the spread in. c(no_pooling_sd = round(sd(ct_fix - mean(ct_fix)), 2),   partial_pooling_sd = round(sd(blup), 2),   sigma_CTmax = round(fit_re$estimates$estimate[fit_re$estimates$parameter == "sigma_CTmax"], 2)) #>      no_pooling_sd partial_pooling_sd        sigma_CTmax  #>               2.32               1.48               1.48

what: Practical guidance: when to reach for which

text: Reach freqTLS want fast, prior-free, asymmetry-respecting intervals; want weak identifiability surfaced rather smoothed ; Stan toolchain impractical. natural default quick, defensible, reproducible fit. Reach bayesTLS genuine prior information use; want full posterior uncertainty (including derived quantities) probability statements; regularisation principled prior exactly thin data call . Use matters. Agreement strong data reassuring; disagreement weak data diagnostic — localises prior work. pluralism, winner, point.

code: set.seed(1) dat <- simulate_tls(family = "beta_binomial", CTmax = 36, z = 4, phi = 50, seed = 1) fit <- fit_tls(dat, y = survived, n = total, time = duration, temp = temp,                family = "beta_binomial", tref = 1)  pc <- profile(fit, "CTmax") c(estimate = pc$estimate, conf.low = pc$conf.low, conf.high = pc$conf.high,   cutoff = pc$cutoff, min_deviance = min(pc$deviance)) #>     estimate     conf.low    conf.high       cutoff min_deviance  #>    36.004832    35.747797    36.268991     3.937117     0.000000 plot(pc)

code: ci_z     <- confint(fit, "z",     method = "profile") ci_log_z <- confint(fit, "log_z", method = "profile")  # z endpoints equal exp() of the log_z endpoints rbind(   z          = c(ci_z$conf.low, ci_z$conf.high),   exp_log_z  = exp(c(ci_log_z$conf.low, ci_log_z$conf.high)) ) #>               [,1]     [,2] #> z         3.428293 4.381551 #> exp_log_z 3.428293 4.381551  # the interval is asymmetric about the estimate (in general) with(ci_z, c(lower_gap = estimate - conf.low, upper_gap = conf.high - estimate)) #> lower_gap upper_gap  #> 0.4730210 0.4802363

what: Recovery when a profile does not close
title: Profile-likelihood intervals
text: headline value-add Bayesian path freqTLS tells data identify parameter, instead letting prior quietly fill gap. profile rise cutoff one side — data sparse pin parameter — default fallback = TRUE first attempts parametric bootstrap. successful bootstrap returns prior-free percentile interval labels method "bootstrap"; stable non-degenerate refits still leave unavailable interval. Set fallback = FALSE specifically need inspect unmodified profile geometry. strict mode freqTLS: emits warning parameter weakly identified (“consider bayesTLS bootstrap”), returns NA open side (never fabricated bound), sets conf.status marker (open_lower, open_upper, open_both). deliberately sparse design — temperatures little mortality contrast — identify CTmax. fit live, show user-facing bootstrap recovery recipe separately. 1,000-refit bootstrap intentionally executed package checks; run displayed chunk interactively need fallback interval. Now disable fallback inspect strict profile. open side remains NA, open_* status, rather becoming confident unsupported bound. Confidence Eye follows contract. default fallback = TRUE, successful bootstrap recovery draws bootstrap lens. strict diagnostic , fallback = FALSE preserves open profile draws hollow point lens.
code: set.seed(7) sparse <- simulate_tls(   temps  = c(35, 36),     # only two temperatures   times  = c(1, 2),       # only two durations   reps   = 2, n = 8,   CTmax  = 36, z = 4,   family = "binomial", seed = 7 ) sparse_fit <- suppressWarnings(   fit_tls(sparse, y = survived, n = total, time = duration, temp = temp,           family = "binomial", tref = 1) ) ci_default <- tryCatch(   withCallingHandlers(     confint(sparse_fit, "CTmax", method = "profile",             fallback = TRUE, nboot = 1000, boot_seed = 7),     warning = function(w) {       message("caught warning: ", conditionMessage(w))       invokeRestart("muffleWarning")     }   ),   error = function(e) e ) ci_default[, c("parameter", "conf.low", "conf.high", "estimate", "method",                "conf.status")] ci_strict <- tryCatch(   withCallingHandlers(     confint(sparse_fit, "CTmax", method = "profile", fallback = FALSE),     warning = function(w) {       message("caught warning: ", conditionMessage(w))       invokeRestart("muffleWarning")     }   ),   error = function(e) e ) #> caught warning: Inner re-optimisation did not converge at 1 grid point while profiling "CTmax". #> ℹ Those points are reported as "NA"; the interval is taken from the points that #>   did converge. #> caught warning: The profile deviance for "CTmax" is non-monotone (multiple local minima). #> ℹ The interval may not be a single connected region; inspect `plot(profile(fit, #>   "CTmax"))`. #> caught warning: The profile likelihood for "CTmax" did not close on the lower and upper sides: #> "CTmax" is weakly identified. #> ℹ Returning "NA" on the open side rather than a fabricated bound. #> ℹ Consider bayesTLS or a bootstrap for this parameter. ci_strict[, c("parameter", "conf.low", "conf.high", "estimate", "method",               "conf.status")] #> # A tibble: 1 × 6 #>   parameter conf.low conf.high estimate method  conf.status #>   <chr>        <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl> <chr>   <chr>       #> 1 CTmax           NA        NA     37.8 profile open_both # fallback = FALSE so the eye refuses to draw a lens when the profile does not # close (a hollow point only), matching the honest non-closing contract above. suppressWarnings(plot_confidence_eye(sparse_fit, parm = "CTmax", method = "profile",                                      fallback = FALSE))

what: Fixed groups vs. a random intercept
title: Random effects: hierarchical thermal tolerance
text: handful well-sampled groups can give fixed CTmax (CTmax ~ group, see vignette("freqTLS")). many groups — groups regard sample population — random intercept CTmax ~ 1 + (1 | group) better tool: costs one variance parameter instead one coefficient per group, group estimates borrow strength . deviations b_g ~ N(0, sigma_CTmax^2) integrated TMB’s Laplace approximation; sigma_CTmax estimated marginal maximum likelihood. formula without (1 | group) uses ordinary fixed-effects objective. Prefer confint(method = "bootstrap") (bayesTLS) fixed effects, describe variance component likely downward-biased rather treating precise estimate literal confidence bound. conditional modes (BLUPs) — colony’s shrunken deviation population CTmax — come ranef(): Prediction distinguishes population curve observed colony’s conditional curve. Population predictions set random intercept zero; conditional predictions add fitted BLUP require grouping column newdata: Calling predict() without re.form random-effects fit warns returns population prediction. unseen group receive estimated BLUP, conditional prediction stops points re.form = "population". confint() routes uncertainty target. gives positive, log-scale Wald interval sigma_CTmax. profiles population CTmax random effect, re-running Laplace approximation grid point, slower fixed-effects profile. fixed-effect profile close, default fallback Wald; request method = "bootstrap" redraw random effects refit full model instead. code: set.seed(1) d <- simulate_tls(family = "binomial", CTmax = 36, z = 4,                   re_sd = 1.5, n_re_groups = 14, seed = 1) fit <- fit_tls(   tls_bf(survived | trials(total) ~ time(duration) + temp(temp),          CTmax ~ 1 + (1 | colony)),   data = d, family = "binomial", tref = 1, quiet = TRUE)  # The estimates table carries the population CTmax / z and the between-colony SD. est <- fit$estimates est[est$parameter %in% c("CTmax", "z", "sigma_CTmax"),     c("parameter", "estimate", "std.error")] #>     parameter  estimate  std.error #> 4       CTmax 36.010947 0.38977872 #> 5           z  3.952015 0.05042902 #> 6 sigma_CTmax  1.454802 0.27579903 head(ranef(fit), 4) #> # A tibble: 4 × 4 #>   group term  estimate std.error #>   <chr> <chr>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 g1    CTmax   -0.906     0.396 #> 2 g10   CTmax   -0.505     0.396 #> 3 g11   CTmax    2.16      0.396 #> 4 g12   CTmax    0.603     0.396 colony_1 <- as.character(ranef(fit)$group[1]) new_colony <- data.frame(temp = 36, duration = 2, colony = colony_1) data.frame(   target = c("population", "colony"),   survival = c(     predict(fit, new_colony, re.form = "population"),     predict(fit, new_colony, re.form = "conditional")   ) ) #>       target   survival #> 1 population 0.19888224 #> 2     colony 0.08525094 suppressMessages(confint(fit, "sigma_CTmax", method = "wald"))[   , c("parameter", "conf.low", "conf.high")] #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>   parameter   conf.low conf.high #>   <chr>          <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 sigma_CTmax     1.00      2.11 suppressMessages(confint(fit, "CTmax", method = "profile", npoints = 8))[   , c("parameter", "conf.low", "conf.high", "method")] #> # A tibble: 1 × 4 #>   parameter conf.low conf.high method  #>   <chr>        <dbl>     <dbl> <chr>   #> 1 CTmax         35.2      36.8 profile suppressMessages(plot_confidence_eye(fit, parm = "CTmax"))

title: Random effects: hierarchical thermal tolerance
text: (1 | group) syntax works log_z (thermal sensitivity) shape coordinates low (lower, long-exposure asymptote) log_k (steepness). One nuance matters: z, low, k modelled transformed scales, variance components live scales — sigma_logz SD log(z), sigma_low logit(low), sigma_logk log(k). Read sigma_logz approximate multiplicative spread z: exp(sigma_logz) fold-change across groups, z-unit SD. upper asymptote one shape random effect: disjoint-bounds parameterisation = up_min + up_w * plogis(beta_up) coordinate beta_up, compiled objective carries random-intercept term (profile path wired either — gap leaves delta-method interval). Put random intercept low log_k instead.
code: set.seed(2) dz <- simulate_tls(family = "binomial", CTmax = 36, z = 4,                    re_sd_z = 0.3, n_re_groups = 14, seed = 2) fit_z <- fit_tls(   tls_bf(survived | trials(total) ~ time(duration) + temp(temp),          log_z ~ 1 + (1 | colony)),   data = dz, family = "binomial", tref = 1, quiet = TRUE) sg <- fit_z$estimates$estimate[fit_z$estimates$parameter == "sigma_logz"] c(sigma_logz = round(sg, 3),   approx_fold_spread_in_z = round(exp(sg), 3)) #>              sigma_logz approx_fold_spread_in_z  #>                   0.265                   1.303

what: Combining random effects, and what is out of scope
title: Random effects: hierarchical thermal tolerance
text: can place random intercepts one coordinate. two share grouping factor, freqTLS fits independent variances (cross-correlations forced zero) warns: real data group-level CTmax z deviations usually correlated; forcing correlation zero, independent-variance fit absorbs marginal SDs fixed-effect intervals. need correlated random effect — crossed/nested grouping factors, random slope — use bayesTLS, whose Stan back end fits full covariance. freqTLS deliberately stops independent random intercepts CTmax / log_z / low / log_k.
code: set.seed(3) db <- simulate_tls(family = "binomial", CTmax = 36, z = 4,                    re_sd = 1.2, re_sd_z = 0.3, n_re_groups = 14, seed = 3) # Both random intercepts on the same `colony` grouping. This fits, but emits a # warning that the two variances are independent (no correlation term); we pass # `quiet = TRUE` to silence it here and discuss it below. fit_both <- fit_tls(   tls_bf(survived | trials(total) ~ time(duration) + temp(temp),          CTmax ~ 1 + (1 | colony), log_z ~ 1 + (1 | colony)),   data = db, family = "binomial", tref = 1, quiet = TRUE) fit_both$estimates$parameter[grepl("^sigma", fit_both$estimates$parameter)] #> [1] "sigma_CTmax" "sigma_logz"

title: freqTLS — frequentist inference for thermal death-time models
text: Fast, prior-free frequentist confidence intervals — Wald, profile-likelihood, bootstrap — thermal-load-sensitivity (thermal death-time) models; maximum-likelihood complement bayesTLS. ⚠️ freqTLS experimental provided without assurance. Use risk. Results APIs may incorrect change. responsible checking data, design, model specification, convergence, identifiability, diagnostics, interpretation. Important analyses independently refitted cross-checked Bayesian sister package bayesTLS (source repository). Agreement cross-check, proof correctness: shared data model errors can make packages agree. freqTLS fits single-stage four-parameter logistic (4PL) thermal death-time model maximum likelihood via TMB, parameterised directly CTmax (critical thermal maximum reference time tref) z (thermal sensitivity, degrees Celsius per decade exposure duration). returns prior-free frequentist confidence intervals — Wald, profile-likelihood (asymmetry-respecting), bootstrap — binomial beta-binomial survival counts, continuous proportion responses (0, 1) via beta family. signature display Confidence Eye: pale horizontal lens spanning selected confidence interval, hollow point estimate. profile- likelihood interval default supported; Wald bootstrap intervals can also shown. likelihood-based confidence intervals, posterior distributions, visual deliberately avoids posterior-density iconography.

title: freqTLS — frequentist inference for thermal death-time models
text: Fits 4PL thermal death-time model maximum likelihood, directly CTmax z, binomial beta-binomial survival counts continuous beta proportions (0, 1) (trials column needed). Inverts likelihood-ratio test give profile-likelihood confidence intervals respect asymmetry carry prior. Surfaces identifiability honestly: profile close, flagged, never fabricated. default confint() falls back prior-free parametric bootstrap; unstable refits remain unavailable rather producing fabricated bound. Set fallback = FALSE keep open profile, Confidence Eye marks hollow point lens. Ships tidy column interface (fit_tls()) brms/drmTMB-style formula interface (tls_bf()) fixed-effect predictors sub-parameter (CTmax, log_z, low, , log_k), plus prediction, lethal-time derivation, plotting (survival curves, survival surface, Confidence Eye). Fits random intercepts CTmax, log_z, low, log_k (<param> ~ <fixed> + (1 | group)), profile intervals fixed effects. one intercept term supported per coordinate, multiple terms remain independent variance blocks; correlated, crossed, nested, random-slope structures implemented. Derives critical temperatures — derive_ctmax() (absolute threshold) derive_tcrit() (rate-multiplier) — predicts heat injury temperature trace (predict_heat_injury()) prior-free bootstrap confidence band (heat_injury_envelope(), plot_heat_injury()). See vignette("frequentist--bayesian") likelihood Bayesian paths compare.

text: workflow follows standardize → fit → quantities → plot sequence bayesTLS, packages drop-replacements. freqTLS implements tested fixed/grouped designs limited random-intercept structures described ; general Bayesian models remain bayesTLS. engine maximum likelihood (Stan, MCMC, internet); uncertainty frequentist trio (Wald, profile, bootstrap) instead posterior. deliberate differences documented vignette("comparing--bayesTLS"): absolute (p-survival) threshold non-default asymptote bounds yet wired ML backbone (fit relative midpoint, convert extract_tdt()); uncertainty comes bootstrap replicates rather posterior draws; temperature effect defaults constant-shape configuration. per-group CTmax z, standardize dataset contains grouping column, pass matching formulas exactly bayesTLS: extract_tdt(), predict_survival_curves(), diagnose_tdt_fit() complete shared-name analogue surface; column / formula engine interface (fit_tls(), tls_bf()) remains available underneath.
code: library(freqTLS)  # 1. Standardize raw survival counts (the shared bayesTLS entry point) dat <- simulate_tls(family = "beta_binomial", CTmax = 36, z = 4, phi = 50, seed = 1) std <- standardize_data(dat, temp = "temp", duration = "duration",                         n_total = "total", n_surv = "survived",                         duration_unit = "hours")  # 2. Fit the 4PL by maximum likelihood, directly in CTmax and z fit <- fit_4pl(std, t_ref = 1)  # 3. Headline thermal-death-time quantities with profile-likelihood intervals tls(fit) #> <tls> relative threshold; quantities: z, CTmax (profile intervals) #> # A tibble: 2 × 4 #>   quantity median lower upper #>   <chr>     <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 CTmax     36.0  35.7  36.3  #> 2 z          3.90  3.43  4.38 grouped_dat <- simulate_tls(   family = "binomial", group = c("cool", "warm"), reps = 4, n = 40,   CTmax = c(35, 38), z = c(4, 4), seed = 2 ) grouped_std <- standardize_data(   grouped_dat, temp = "temp", duration = "duration",   n_total = "total", n_surv = "survived" ) grouped_fit <- fit_4pl(   grouped_std, ctmax = ~ 0 + group, z = ~ 0 + group, t_ref = 1 ) # Inspect the recovered group-specific CTmax and z estimates. tidy_parameters(grouped_fit)[   grepl("^(CTmax|z):", tidy_parameters(grouped_fit)$parameter),   c("parameter", "estimate") ] # 4. Plot the fitted survival surface (the Confidence Eye is shown above) plot_survival_curves(fit)

code: # 15 colonies whose CTmax varies with SD = 1.5 C. dre <- simulate_tls(family = "binomial", CTmax = 36, z = 4,                     re_sd = 1.5, n_re_groups = 15, seed = 1) fit_re <- fit_tls(   tls_bf(survived | trials(total) ~ time(duration) + temp(temp),          CTmax ~ 1 + (1 | colony)),   data = dre, family = "binomial", tref = 1 ) # Between-colony SD of CTmax (with a Wald interval): tp <- tidy_parameters(fit_re) tp[tp$parameter == "sigma_CTmax", c("parameter", "estimate", "conf.low", "conf.high")] #> # A tibble: 1 × 4 #>   parameter   estimate conf.low conf.high #>   <chr>          <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 sigma_CTmax     1.48     1.03      2.12  # Colony BLUPs (deviations from the population CTmax): head(ranef(fit_re), 3) #> # A tibble: 3 × 4 #>   group term  estimate std.error #>   <chr> <chr>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 g1    CTmax   -1.03      0.390 #> 2 g10   CTmax   -0.651     0.390 #> 3 g11   CTmax    2.05      0.390  # Choose the prediction target explicitly for a random-intercept fit. colony_1 <- as.character(ranef(fit_re)$group[1]) new_re <- data.frame(temp = 36, duration = 2, colony = colony_1) data.frame(   target = c("population", "colony"),   survival = c(     predict(fit_re, new_re, re.form = "population"),     predict(fit_re, new_re, re.form = "conditional")   ) ) #>       target   survival #> 1 population 0.22405366 #> 2     colony 0.08768286

code: # The tolerant population has a higher CTmax and a steeper survival collapse. dpop <- simulate_tls(family = "binomial", group = c("tolerant", "sensitive"),                      CTmax = c(38, 35), z = c(3.5, 3.5),                      low = 0.02, up = 0.98, k = c(8, 3), seed = 11) fit_pop <- fit_tls(   tls_bf(survived | trials(total) ~ time(duration) + temp(temp),          CTmax ~ group, log_z ~ group,          low ~ group, up ~ group, log_k ~ group),   data = dpop, family = "binomial", tref = 1 ) # Per-group steepness k with profile-likelihood confidence intervals: confint(fit_pop, parm = c("k:tolerant", "k:sensitive"), method = "profile") #> # A tibble: 2 × 8 #>   parameter   conf.low conf.high estimate level method  scale conf.status #>   <chr>          <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <chr>   <chr> <chr>       #> 1 k:tolerant      7.43     10.6      8.86  0.95 profile log   ok          #> 2 k:sensitive     2.40      3.48     2.90  0.95 profile log   ok plot_survival_curves(fit_pop)

title: freqTLS — frequentist inference for thermal death-time models
text: exposure duration d (logd = log10(d)) assay temperature T, survival follows descending four-parameter logistic p=low+−low1+exp⁡(k(log⁡10d−mid)),mid=log⁡10(tref)−T−CTmaxz. p = \mathrm{low} + \frac{\mathrm{} - \mathrm{low}}{1 + \exp\!\big(k\,(\log_{10} d - \mathrm{mid})\big)}, \qquad \mathrm{mid} = \log_{10}(t_\mathrm{ref}) - \frac{T - \mathrm{CTmax}}{z}. CTmax critical thermal maximum reference time tref, z thermal sensitivity (degrees Celsius per decade exposure duration). default constant-shape configuration, temperature effect runs midpoint (shared low, , k). Explicit formula terms can instead model low, , log_k; extensions change interpretation absolute-threshold curve create local z estimates. CTmax z direct model parameters, can profiled directly. NULL (default) returns intervals natural-scale parameters fit. level Confidence level (default 0.95). method One "profile" (default), "wald", "bootstrap". npoints Number grid points used per profile (default 30); ignored method = "wald" method = "bootstrap". trace Logical; print inner-optimisation progress (profile/bootstrap). fallback Logical; method = "profile" (default), fall back parametric bootstrap parameter whose profile close, parameters fit's Hessian positive definite (pdHess = FALSE). Default TRUE. nboot Number bootstrap replicates method = "bootstrap" fallback (default 1000). boot_seed Optional integer seed making bootstrap reproducible without disturbing caller's random stream (default NULL). cores Number CPU cores bootstrap refits (default 1, maximum 2). Requests two warn use two. cores > 1 refits replicates parallel forking (Unix; sequential Windows). Results identical given boot_seed regardless cores. ... Reserved; must empty.

title: Confidence intervals for a fitted thermal-load-sensitivity model — confint.profile_tls
text: method = "profile" (default) computes profile-likelihood confidence intervals inverting likelihood-ratio test: interval {psi : D(psi) <= qt(1 - alpha/2, df)^2}, found stats::uniroot() side MLE unconstrained internal coordinate, endpoints transformed natural scale. cutoff squared profile-t quantile df = n - p residual degrees freedom (Bates-Watts profile-t), qchisq(level, 1); two coincide df -> Inf. intervals prior-free respect asymmetry. equivariant monotone reparameterisation, z interval equals exp() internal log_z interval. method = "wald" reuses Phase-2 Wald path: estimate +/- t * se (t = qt(1 - alpha/2, df) df = n - p) internal (link) scale, back-transformed. method = "bootstrap" returns prior-free parametric-bootstrap percentile intervals: survival counts regenerated observed design fitted 4PL, model refitted nboot times, interval percentile range replicate estimates. frequentist resampling interval, posterior credible interval. returns finite interval enough stable, non-degenerate refits remain. profile close one side, fitted Hessian positive definite (pdHess = FALSE), confint() falls back parametric bootstrap affected parameters (message). fallback can still return NA valid refits remain. Set fallback = FALSE keep strict profile behaviour, returns NA open side (never fabricated bound) warning parameter weakly identified (see vignette("profile-likelihood")). upper asymptote coordinate beta_up disjoint bounds yet profiled, reported delta-method Wald interval profile/Wald methods, message. fit random intercept (CTmax ~ <fixed> + (1 | group)), method = "profile" profiles fixed-effect coordinates re-running Laplace approximation grid point, slower fixed-effects profile. Variance components keep log-scale Wald intervals profile method, non-closing random-effects profile falls back Wald. method = "bootstrap" instead redraws every active random-intercept block refits Laplace approximation, returning percentile intervals enough stable refits remain.

title: Derive the temperature giving a target survival at a fixed exposure — derive_ctmax

Supplying absolute surv gives absolute- threshold critical temperature (analogue bayesTLS extract_tdt() absolute mode), asymmetry correction qlogis((surv - low) / (- low)) / k built .

title: Derive the temperature giving a target survival at a fixed exposure — derive_ctmax
text: object profile_tls fit fit_tls(). surv Target survival probability (low, ). NULL (default) uses relative midpoint (low + ) / 2, reproducing CTmax tref. duration Exposure duration(s) (native time unit; strictly positive). Defaults fit's tref. group Optional single group level (grouped fits ).

title: Derive the temperature giving a target survival at a fixed exposure — derive_ctmax
text: numeric vector temperatures (degrees C), one per duration.

title: Derive the temperature giving a target survival at a fixed exposure — derive_ctmax
text: Solving surv = low + (- low) * plogis(-k (log10(duration) - mid)) mid = log10(tref) - (temp - CTmax) / z temperature gives $$temp = CTmax - z\Big(\log_{10} duration - \log_{10} t_{ref} +   \mathrm{qlogis}\!\big(\tfrac{surv - low}{- low}\big) / k\Big).$$ target surv must lie strictly low . random-effects fit population-level derived quantity; add group BLUP.

title: Derive the lethal / survival-threshold duration at a temperature — derive_lt

code: d <- simulate_tls(family = "binomial", CTmax = 36, z = 4, seed = 1) fit <- fit_tls(d, y = survived, n = total, time = duration, temp = temp,                family = "binomial", tref = 1) nd <- expand.grid(temp = c(34, 36, 38), duration = c(1, 2, 4)) predict(fit, nd, type = "survival") #> [1] 0.89445180 0.47691987 0.09003950 0.69738630 0.18571146 0.03695279 0.36162991 #> [8] 0.06378846 0.02386996  # A continuous predictor used by CTmax and log_z must also be in newdata. d$x <- rep(c(-1, 1), length.out = nrow(d)) fit_x <- fit_tls(   tls_bf(survived | trials(total) ~ time(duration) + temp(temp),          CTmax ~ x, log_z ~ x),   data = d, family = "binomial", tref = 1 ) predict(fit_x, data.frame(temp = 36, duration = 2, x = c(-1, 1))) #> [1] 0.1678129 0.2048208  # \donttest{ # Choose population or fitted-group prediction explicitly for an RE fit. dre <- simulate_tls(family = "binomial", CTmax = 36, z = 4,                     re_sd = 1, n_re_groups = 8, seed = 2) fit_re <- fit_tls(   tls_bf(survived | trials(total) ~ time(duration) + temp(temp),          CTmax ~ 1 + (1 | colony)),   data = dre, family = "binomial", tref = 1 ) colony <- as.character(ranef(fit_re)$group[1]) nd_re <- data.frame(temp = 36, duration = 2, colony = colony) predict(fit_re, nd_re, re.form = "population") #> [1] 0.2124208 predict(fit_re, nd_re, re.form = "conditional") #> [1] 0.1003736 # }

title: Predict cumulative heat injury under a temperature trace — predict_heat_injury
text: object profile_tls fit fit_tls(), freq_tls workflow fit_4pl(). trace data frame numeric columns time (strictly increasing, least two rows) temp (degrees C). group Optional single group level (grouped fits ; required fit grouped). target_surv Optional absolute survival threshold defining one lethal dose: single probability strictly fitted lower upper asymptotes. NULL (default) uses project-default relative threshold (curve midpoint (low + ) / 2), matching derive_ctmax() derive_lt(). bootstrap envelope target must also attainable every converged bootstrap refit; otherwise function aborts rather clipping invalid refit's threshold. t_c Optional damage-cutoff temperature (degrees C): damage rate zero. NULL (default) applies cutoff. repair Optional named list Sharpe-Schoolfield repair parameters (see Details); NULL (default) means repair. irreversible Logical; TRUE (default) survival monotone non-increasing (mortality reverse even dose repaired).

title: Predict cumulative heat injury under a temperature trace — predict_heat_injury
text: data frame columns time, temp, dose (cumulative, fraction lethal dose), injury (dose * 100, percent), survival.

title: Predict cumulative heat injury under a temperature trace — predict_heat_injury
text: One lethal dose thermal load drives survival target set target_surv. default target_surv = NULL target project-default relative threshold – curve midpoint (low + ) / 2. temperature T lethal time target LT(T) = tref * 10^((CTmax - T) / z - q / k), q = qlogis((target_surv - low) / (- low)) (q = 0 midpoint, quantity derive_lt() survival target_surv). instantaneous damage rate 1 / LT(T) (lethal doses per time unit). Cumulative dose accumulated forward Euler trace, using actual per-step time increments: $$D_j = \max\!\big(0,\; D_{j-1} + (\mathrm{dmg}(T_{j-1}) -   \mathrm{rep}_{j-1})\,\Delta t_j\big),\quad D_1 = 0,$$ \(\Delta t_j = t_j - t_{j-1}\). Survival read back 4PL treating accumulated dose equivalent log10-time: survival(D) = low + (- low) * plogis(-k * log10(D) + q), D = 1 (one lethal dose) reaches exactly target_surv – relative midpoint (low + ) / 2 default, absolute survival threshold target_surv supplied. trace time fit's duration / tref must share time unit (damage rate per unit). irreversible = TRUE (default) survival monotone non-increasing. damage cutoff t_c (example derive_tcrit()) sets damage rate zero t_c. Heat-injury prediction currently requires shared fixed-effect shape formulas; varying shape need re-evaluated along temperature trace. integrator forward Euler (left-endpoint, per actual step), single-dt scheme implementations use; irregular traces integrated real increments.

text: data Raw data frame tibble. temp Column name assay temperature (°C). duration Column name exposure duration. unit whatever source data; record via duration_unit. n_total Column name total individuals per replicate. Required count responses; leave NULL (default) continuous proportion response. n_surv Column name survivor counts. n_dead Column name death counts. Converted n_surv via n_surv = n_total - n_dead. survival Column name survival proportions [0, 1]. Converted integer counts via n_total. mortality Column name mortality proportions [0, 1]. Converted n_surv = round((1 - mortality) * n_total). proportion Column name continuous proportion response [0, 1] denominator (modelled Beta likelihood). Mutually exclusive count arguments . proportion_eps Boundary clamp applied proportion values sit strictly inside (0, 1) (Beta density undefined exactly 0 1). Default 0.001. random_effects Optional character vector grouping variables random effects, e.g. c("Date", "Tank"). columns converted factors stored metadata fitter read. duration_unit Label unit duration, stored metadata. Default "hours". temp_mean Value subtract temp form temp_c. NULL (default) uses mean(temp). Supply fixed value align multiple datasets common centre.

title: Standardise a raw survival / proportion dataset for the TDT function library — standardize_data
text: tibble standardised columns plus "tdt_meta" attribute storing temp_mean, duration_unit, random_effects, response_type ("count" "proportion"), response_var (response column name proportion fit, else NULL), proportion_eps (clamp used proportion fit, else NULL).

title: Standardise a raw survival / proportion dataset for the TDT function library — standardize_data
text: Two response types supported: Count data (binomial / beta-binomial): supply n_total plus exactly one n_surv, n_dead, survival, mortality. counts derived standardised columns include n_total, n_surv, n_dead, survival. response_type recorded "count". Continuous proportion (Beta), e.g. chlorophyll-fluorescence \(F_v/F_m\) ratio denominator: supply proportion omit count arguments. value stored survival (clamped open interval (proportion_eps, 1 - proportion_eps) Beta likelihood finite). warning reports affected count epsilon whenever values changed; n_total/n_surv columns created. response_type recorded "proportion". dataset spans multiple categories (life stages, species, populations, etc.), retain grouping column. Use fit_4pl(= "group") grouped formulas tls_bf() estimate category-level effects, filter first separate models scientifically preferable.

title: Build a freqTLS formula object (brms/drmTMB-style) — tls_bf
text: first argument must unnamed response--axes formula. left-hand side names survival counts, either brms idiom successes | trials(total) glm idiom cbind(successes, failures). right-hand side names two thermal-load-sensitivity axes tagged markers time(<duration>) temp(<temperature>) (order matter). remaining arguments sub-parameter formulas keyed left-hand side, one low, , log_k, CTmax, log_z. sub-parameter omit defaults ~ 1. low, , log_k may carry design independently — grouping factor (low ~ group), continuous covariate (log_k ~ body_size), intercept — need share one factor match headline-parameter grouping. CTmax log_z accept fixed-effect formulas must produce model-matrix columns (example, use CTmax ~ group, log_z ~ group); supported random- intercept groupings may differ. single random intercept, <param> ~ <fixed> + (1 | group), accepted CTmax, log_z, low, log_k (one grouping factor , intercept ) – upper asymptote , compiled objective random-intercept term. Putting grouping factor two fits independent variances (correlation term) warns.

title: Response families for thermal-load-sensitivity models — tls_family
text: phi convention beta-binomial family sum Beta shape parameters: fitted survival probability p, counts Beta-Binomial shapes = p * phi b = (1 - p) * phi. Larger phi means less overdispersion (binomial recovered phi grows). matches simulation convention simulate_tls() differs precision/size parameterisations used packages. beta family uses shapes continuous proportion, y ~ Beta(p * phi, (1 - p) * phi), phi carries identical meaning larger phi means tighter response around fitted curve.

what: Stage 2 of the classical two-stage TDT pipeline — ts_stage2
title: Stage 2 of the classical two-stage TDT pipeline — ts_stage2
text: Regresses valid Stage-1 log10(LT50) estimates assay temperature ordinary least squares derives classical quantities. z = -1/slope; CTmax(t_ref) = (log10(t_ref) - intercept) / slope; T_crit follows rate-multiplier definition, CTmax + z * mean(log10(TC_rate_range/100)). t_ref expressed minutes recorded returned $settings. Thus summary$CTmax always CTmax explicitly recorded reference duration, necessarily CTmax one hour. Stage 2 retains rows chosen rows; fewer three valid Stage-1 temperatures remain, returns NULL fit NA summary rather extrapolating TDT line.

TC_rate_range Length-2 HI-rate range (% per hour) T_crit. rows Stage-1 rows keep: "stage1_ok" (bracketing validation, case-study default) "finite_ok" (finite/negative slope , simulation's looser rule).
